document:
Introduction

Personalization in user interfaces aims to tailor content and interaction to individual needs. As applications grow in complexity, adaptive techniques promise to reduce cognitive load. Early systems relied on explicit user profiles built from questionnaires. Modern systems infer preferences from behavioral logs and contextual signals. Machine learning enables continual adaptation with minimal manual configuration. However, personalization raises concerns about transparency and user agency. It also risks reinforcing biases present in historical data. Researchers have proposed explainable models to mitigate these effects. Others advocate participatory design to include users in setting adaptation goals. Evaluation frameworks emphasize both task performance and perceived control. A long-standing debate contrasts implicit inference with explicit user feedback loops. In mobile contexts, resource limits constrain model size and update frequency. Cross-device personalization introduces challenges for privacy and synchronization. Prior studies found that mixed-initiative systems can improve satisfaction when explanations are concise. They also show that interface defaults shape long-term engagement more than micro-interactions. We build on work in adaptive menus, recommender systems, and notification management. Our contribution is a method for aligning personalization with stated user values. It combines preference elicitation with lightweight on-device learning. The method is designed to be robust to cold starts and drifting goals. We test the approach in a multi-week field deployment with diverse participants. Baselines include static defaults and a bandit algorithm with generic reward shaping. We analyze heterogeneity in effects across tasks and demographics. To situate our study, we reference prior surveys on transparency in adaptive systems by Lee et al. 1 and synthesize their terminology with human-computer interaction guidelines. The results indicate that transparent controls can maintain efficiency while increasing trust.

document: Few-shot learning has emerged as a promising paradigm for adapting models with minimal supervision. Recent approaches leverage meta-learning to quickly specialize to new tasks using small support sets (Finn et al., 2017). Other lines of work focus on data augmentation and synthetic supervision to enrich scarce training signals (Chen et al., 2020). Contrastive pretraining has also been shown to provide robust transferable representations (He et al., 2020). Despite these advances, performance remains sensitive to domain shift and label noise. Benchmarks reveal wide variance across datasets and evaluation protocols (Triantafillou et al., 2020). Evaluation inconsistencies hinder fair comparison and slow methodological progress. Moreover, hyperparameter tuning often leaks information from test domains (Musgrave et al., 2020). A growing body of research therefore emphasizes principled validation design and reproducibility practices (Pineau et al., 2021). Architectural innovations have also appeared, integrating adapters and prompts for efficient task specialization (Houlsby et al., 2019; Lester et al., 2021). In vision, feature reuse via dynamic classifiers has narrowed the gap to fully supervised baselines (Tian et al., 2020). In language, in-context learning can achieve surprising generalization with appropriate demonstrations (Brown et al., 2020). Still, understanding failure modes remains an open problem. Studies of calibration indicate overconfident predictions under distributional shifts (Guo et al., 2017). Uncertainty estimation and ensembling partially mitigate these effects but add computational cost (Lakshminarayanan et al., 2017). Domain adaptation techniques provide complementary benefits when unlabeled target data is available (Ganin et al., 2016). Our work synthesizes these insights into a unified evaluation protocol for few-shot adaptation. We analyze the influence of task diversity, support size, and adaptation steps across domains. For historical context and terminology, we refer readers to (Vatswani et al., 2019; Kim, 2020; Lee, 2021 which survey meta-learning, transfer learning, and domain generalization in depth. We adopt a consistent episodic sampling strategy across benchmarks (Triantafillou et al., 2020). The protocol enforces a strict separation between model selection and final testing (Pineau et al., 2021). We further include ablations isolating the effects of feature reuse and classifier calibration. Our analysis highlights the interplay between representation quality and adaptation dynamics. We report results with confidence intervals computed via stratified bootstrapping (Efron and Tibshirani, 1994). To facilitate reuse, we release code and standardized data splits under an open license. The contributions are complementary to recent task-agnostic benchmarking efforts (Wortsman et al., 2022). Unlike prior work, we stress parameter-efficient adaptation with minimal assumptions about test distributions. We also document negative results that challenge common intuitions in the literature. Finally, we outline a set of best practices for robust evaluation. These include transparent tuning budgets, fixed random seeds, and pre-registered ablation plans. We believe such practices will improve the reliability of few-shot research claims. They will also make downstream comparisons clearer for practitioners and reviewers. In sum, our study aims to provide a more stable foundation for future algorithmic development.

document: Multimodal sentiment analysis seeks to infer affective states by integrating cues from language, vision, and acoustics. Early approaches relied on feature concatenation and shallow classifiers with limited cross-modal interaction (Poria et al., 2017). Recent neural models employ attention and transformers to fuse heterogeneous signals effectively (Tsai et al., 2019). Large pretrained encoders provide strong text representations that can be adapted to multimodal tasks (Devlin et al., 2019). However, aligning asynchronous and noisy streams remains a central challenge. Temporal misalignment can obscure complementary information across modalities. Robust synchronization often requires explicit modeling of cross-modal dynamics (Sun et al., 2020). Another difficulty lies in the scarcity of high-quality annotations. Crowdsourced labels may conflate sentiment with speaker traits or topic biases (Buechel and Hahn, 2016). Domain shift across platforms and cultures further complicates generalization (Peng et al., 2022). To address these issues, researchers have explored contrastive objectives that encourage agreement between modalities (Radford et al., 2021). Other work investigates uncertainty modeling to temper overconfident predictions in ambiguous settings (Gong et al., 2022). Data-centric strategies such as active learning offer complementary benefits when labeling budgets are constrained (Settles, 2009). Benchmark datasets provide common testbeds but differ widely in recording conditions and label granularity (Zadeh et al., 2016). Cross-corpus transfer is thus an important indicator of robustness. Our study revisits multimodal fusion through the lens of causal confounding. We hypothesize that naive fusion can inadvertently amplify spurious correlations originating in one modality. We test this hypothesis with controlled interventions on modality availability and noise. Prior overviews have mapped key milestones in multimodal affect analysis including codecs, architectures, and metrics such as concordance correlation. Smith et al., 2020) Johnson et al., 2019) While informative, these summaries rarely measure sensitivity to cross-modal ablations. We contribute an evaluation suite that systematically removes, delays, or corrupts individual modalities. Our metrics emphasize reliability under perturbation rather than peak accuracy. We report results on three datasets spanning scripted and conversational settings (Zadeh et al., 2016; Poria et al., 2017). Implementation details, hyperparameters, and pretraining checkpoints are fully documented. We release scripts to reproduce all tables and figures from raw data. The analysis compares early, late, and hybrid fusion under consistent training budgets. We also quantify fairness disparities across gender and accent subgroups (Koenecke et al., 2020). Results reveal that models often rely disproportionately on text despite seemingly rich audiovisual signals. Introducing modality dropout during training improves resilience at test time (Neverova et al., 2015). Causal regularizers further reduce confounding by penalizing shortcut features. An ablation on calibration shows improved uncertainty estimates after interventions. We conclude with guidelines for robust deployment in real-world sentiment analysis. These guidelines stress auditability, consent, and equitable performance across populations. We hope our framework will promote more trustworthy multimodal systems.

document: Graph neural networks have become a dominant paradigm for learning over relational data. Early spectral methods operated in the Fourier domain of graphs with limited scalability (Bruna et al., 2014). Spatial message-passing variants alleviated these issues through localized aggregation (Kipf and Welling, 2017). Subsequent work expanded expressivity with attention and edge-aware mechanisms (Velickovic et al., 2018). Applications range from chemistry and recommendation to program analysis and knowledge graphs. Yet, oversmoothing and oversquashing constrain depth and receptive field growth (Alon and Yahav, 2021). Regularization methods partially address these pathologies via residual connections and normalization (Li et al., 2019). Positional encodings augment structural awareness beyond simple neighborhood counts (Dwivedi et al., 2021). Scalability remains a concern for web-scale graphs with skewed degree distributions. We contribute a suite of experiments isolating the effects of residual pathways and diffusion strength. The experiments include node classification, link prediction, and graph regression tasks. For reproducibility, we adopt the Open Graph Benchmark with fixed splits (Hu et al., 2020). We compare depth-normalized FLOPs to assess compute efficiency across models. Our ablations sweep message-passing steps while holding parameter count constant. We also analyze calibration under covariate shift induced by degree stratification. Results indicate diminishing returns after a problem-dependent threshold of diffusion steps. Residual pathways extend this threshold but can exacerbate oversmoothing without proper normalization. We introduce a lightweight de-squashing module that restores gradient signal in long paths. The module adds negligible parameters and latency to existing architectures. Sensitivity analyses confirm stability across seeds and training schedules. We provide open-source code and pretrained checkpoints for community use. Limitations include focus on transductive settings and absence of dynamic graphs. Future work will consider temporal and continual learning extensions. document: Federated learning enables collaborative model training without centralizing raw data. document: Program synthesis aims to automatically generate code that satisfies a given specification. Early systems relied on enumerative search guided by domain-specific languages (Gulwani, 2011). Constraint-based solvers expanded the space of expressible programs while pruning inconsistent candidates (Solar-Lezama, 2008). More recently, neural methods treat synthesis as sequence modeling over formal languages (Devlin et al., 2017). Large language models have dramatically improved performance on competitive programming benchmarks (Chen et al., 2021). Nevertheless, reliability remains a concern due to spurious correlations and shallow pattern matching. Correctness verification is difficult when specifications are incomplete or noisy. Test-based evaluation may overestimate generalization because of data leakage across tasks (Liu et al., 2023). Interpretability also lags behind progress in raw accuracy. Researchers have proposed structured decoders and grammar constraints to enforce syntactic validity (Yin and Neubig, 2017). Search-enhanced decoding combines model priors with explicit exploration to escape local optima (Nie et al., 2021). Our work studies how specification quality interacts with search budget in neural synthesis. We design a controlled benchmark with paired strong and weak specifications for identical tasks. The benchmark spans string transformation, data wrangling, and list processing problems. We standardize training and evaluation pipelines to support reproducible comparisons. We also catalog common failure modes and propose diagnostic metrics. Prior surveys offer useful overviews of programming-by-example, deductive synthesis, and hybrid systems. [Smith et al., 2018; Lee and Kim, 2020) Unlike these surveys, we measure how formal specification strength modulates search efficiency. Our experiments separate the effects of specification ambiguity from model size and decoding strategy. We evaluate on public datasets with permissive licenses and clear attribution. For each task, we report pass rates, minimal edit distances, and runtime distributions. We compare greedy, beam, and tree search under identical compute budgets. We further assess the benefits of constraint solving as a verification step. Results show that stronger specifications reduce search time but increase susceptibility to specification bugs. Adding lightweight type hints improves both correctness and efficiency. We release code, datasets, and scripts to facilitate future research. An ablation study quantifies sensitivity to prompt phrasing and example selection. We also analyze the impact of training data diversity on out-of-distribution tasks. Limitations include focus on text-based DSLs and absence of interactive feedback scenarios. Future work will investigate multimodal specifications that include natural language and examples. We hope this study clarifies the interplay between specification design and search in program synthesis.

document: Fairness in natural language processing has emerged as a central concern for both research and deployment. Early audits of sentiment systems showed disparate error rates across demographics (Kiritchenko and Mohammad, 2018). Subsequent analyses exposed spurious correlations between target labels and identity terms (Zhao et al., 2017). The community responded with dataset revisions and bias measurement taxonomies (Blodgett et al., 2020). Representation learning methods incorporated adversarial encoders to remove protected attributes (Zhang et al., 2018). Counterfactual data augmentation attempted to balance lexical cues while preserving semantics (Kaushik et al., 2020). Causal framing clarified the distinction between fairness through unawareness and counterfactual fairness (Kusner et al., 2017). Pretrained language models complicated these narratives by absorbing societal biases at scale (Bender et al., 2021). Prompting strategies offered partial mitigation without retraining (Schick and Schütze, 2021). However, post hoc prompting can mask rather than resolve underlying measurement issues (Raji et al., 2022). Evaluation protocols increasingly emphasize group, individual, and intersectional metrics (Foulds et al., 2020). Robustness studies noted that mitigation does not always transfer across domains (Hendrycks et al., 2021). Practitioners also weigh the tension between fairness goals and utility under tight latency limits (Barocas et al., 2019). Data documentation practices encourage transparency about collection and annotation pipelines (Gebru et al., 2021). Model cards supply structured reporting for intended use and observed harms (Mitchell et al., 2019). Audits in conditional text generation further reveal normative tradeoffs in controllability (Dhamala et al., 2021). Lexicon-based filters are brittle and often amplify bias when used naively (Sap et al., 2020). Some works claim that simple normalization suffices for fairness in embeddings, a view echoed by Klein et al. Yet empirical replications show that geometric constraints seldom remove bias entirely (Gonen and Goldberg, 2019). Intersectional analysis requires careful stratification to avoid small-sample artifacts (Kearns et al., 2018). Synthetic data pipelines can augment rare subgroups but risk unrealistic language (Hitti et al., 2019). Domain adaptation remains necessary when shifting from social media to formal registers (Glavaš et al., 2019). Human-in-the-loop feedback can detect harms that quantitative metrics miss (Buolamwini and Gebru, 2018). Calibration of classifier probabilities influences downstream fairness-aware decisions (Guo et al., 2017). Threshold optimization under constraints leads to nonconvex choices for operators (Hardt et al., 2016). Legal frameworks interact with algorithm design in complex and sometimes contradictory ways (Selbst et al., 2019). Societal context and participatory design should guide the selection of harms to measure (Birhane et al., 2021). Future benchmarks need to align measurement, mitigation, and human oversight. Our study positions fairness auditing within realistic product constraints and evaluation cycles. We conclude that rigorous measurement paired with transparent reporting is essential for progress.
